architecture: Standard layout: cmd/<app> for entrypoints, internal/ for private packages, pkg/ for reusable libs.
Layering: handler/transport -> service (business logic) -> repository (data) -> store (db/http).
Dependency injection via constructor functions returning interfaces; wire in main.
context.Context is the first parameter of any call that does I/O or can be cancelled.
Accept interfaces, return structs; define interfaces in the consumer package, not the producer.
conventions: Wrap errors with fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err); never discard errors; do not panic in library code.
Return errors, do not log-and-continue; log once at the boundary.
Zero-value usable structs where possible; avoid unnecessary pointers.
Keep functions small; early-return on error instead of nesting.
Exported identifiers have doc comments starting with the identifier name.
lint: Format with gofmt/goimports; code must be gofmt-clean.
Run go vet and golangci-lint; fix issues within the changed scope.
No unused variables/imports; no naked returns in long functions.
Run go test ./... ; prefer table-driven tests.
templates: scaffold-data: define a model struct (json tags, pointers for nullable fields), a Repository interface + implementation, and mapping to/from the store.
scaffold-screen (HTTP endpoint): request/response DTO structs, a handler function, and route registration on the router; validate input, return typed errors.
Do not invent a new layout — mirror the existing cmd/internal structure.
design-system: API contract: use one consistent JSON response envelope across endpoints.
Map errors to correct HTTP status codes; return a uniform error response shape.
Version the API in the path or header; keep request/response DTOs separate from domain models.
Document endpoints (OpenAPI/Swagger) as the source of truth.
library-docs: Prefer the standard library first (net/http, encoding/json, database/sql, log/slog).
Router: chi / gin / echo — follow whichever the repo already uses; do not mix.
DB: sqlx or an existing ORM (gorm) — match the repo; logging via slog/zap consistently.
